food_subgroup: Italian charcuterie and fresh cheese platter (antipasto)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite Italian affettati-and-burrata platter. FOODON_03315116 (charcuterie food product) is the closest defensible parent covering the cured-meat component. Burrata cheese component is not separately coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (composite meat-based mixed dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for an Italian affettati-and-burrata antipasto platter in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this composite Italian charcuterie and fresh cheese platter.

main_ingredients: Salty Fried Bread (crostini or fried focaccia)
Burrata D.O.P (fresh mozzarella-style cheese with cream filling)

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared antipasto platter intended for 2–4 persons, estimated at approximately 450 g total weight including cured meats, burrata, fried bread, and pickled vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Prosciutto (NDB 07027), Mortadella (NDB 07050), Smoked pork (NDB 10124), Italian salami (NDB 07064), Fried bread (NDB 18023), Pickled vegetables (NDB 11940), Burrata / fresh mozzarella (NDB 01028)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this composite platter. Per-100 g values are weighted-average estimates across the seven component foods using approximate platter proportions: Parma Ham 15%, Mortadella 15%, Speck 12%, Salami 12%, Fried Bread 16%, Pickled Vegetables 10%, Burrata 20%. Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors. Sodium is notably high due to cured-meat components.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-and-cheese antipasto platter estimated from analogous components: cured meats (GI ~0), fresh cheese/burrata (GI ~0), fried bread/crostini (GI ~70 at 16% weight contribution), pickled vegetables (GI ~15 at 10% weight contribution). Weighted carbohydrate-contribution method applied.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (component anchors) — analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite platter. GI estimated at 45 using carbohydrate-weighted averaging of components: cured meats contribute negligible carbohydrate (GI effectively 0); burrata contributes negligible carbohydrate (GI effectively 0); fried bread (crostini) contributes the dominant glycemic load (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database white bread analogue); pickled vegetables contribute minor carbohydrate (GI ~15). Weighted composite GI ≈ 45, classified low (≤55). GL per 450 g serving = (45 × 10.5 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 21.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~980 mg per 100 g) from multiple cured and smoked meats
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its very high sodium load from Parma Ham, Mortadella, Speck, and Salami; a single shared serving can deliver well over 2,000 mg sodium, substantially exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~45) with meaningful carbohydrate contribution from fried bread component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The platter has a low-to-moderate GI of approximately 45, but the fried bread component contributes meaningful carbohydrate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size, prioritise the protein- and fat-rich cured meat and burrata components, and limit the fried bread to control postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~7.4 g/100 g), high sodium (~980 mg/100 g), and processed meat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This platter is high in both saturated fat and sodium from processed and cured meats, and regular consumption of processed meats is associated with increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food and keep portion sizes small.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Affettati Burrata Platter is an Italian antipasto sharing board featuring a curated selection of premium cured and smoked meats alongside fresh Burrata D.O.P cheese. The platter typically includes Aged Parma Ham (Prosciutto di Parma), Pistachio Mortadella, Smoked Tiroler Speck, and Finocchiona (a fennel-seed Italian salami), accompanied by Salty Fried Bread (crostini or fried focaccia), Pickled Vegetables (giardiniera), and a whole or halved Burrata D.O.P. The cured meats are served thinly sliced and cold; the Burrata is served at room temperature to allow its creamy stracciatella interior to flow when cut. The flavour profile is complex: savoury, smoky, and slightly spiced from the meats, rich and milky from the Burrata, tangy from the pickled vegetables, and crisp and salty from the fried bread. Nutritionally, the platter is high in protein (~16 g/100 g) and fat (~20 g/100 g), with a significant saturated fat contribution from both the cured meats and the Burrata cream filling. Sodium content is very high (~980 mg/100 g) due to the salt-curing and smoking processes applied to the meats. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), with the fried bread being the primary glycemic contributor. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat load, and of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the fried bread carbohydrate contribution. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains milk, gluten, and tree nut (pistachio) allergens. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk as a premium shared starter.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Italian charcuterie?
Can diabetics eat Italian cold cuts?
What meats are in an affettati board?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite platter. Nutrient values are weighted-average estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each component food using approximate platter proportions. GI is an analogous estimate using carbohydrate-weighted component GI values anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate and cannot be reliably attributed to the platter as a whole. All other -1 fields were resolved using USDA component data. Nutrient values represent a per-100 g weighted average across seven platter components at the proportions stated in nutrient_source. Actual restaurant portion nutrient content will vary with platter composition and serving size. The platter is served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore (field survey listing). Price SGD 61.04. Finocchiona is described as 'spicy' in the menu listing; traditional Finocchiona is fennel-flavoured and mildly spiced rather than hot. Burrata D.O.P designation noted from menu; Burrata di Andria IGP is the most common D.O.P/IGP-protected Italian burrata. GI estimated at 45 (low, ≤55 threshold) using carbohydrate-weighted component method; this is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement.
